text:
If you're as good with numbers as you are people--and in the New York area-- I cannot recommend applying to this job more strongly!"
"
"Jaya Pokuri and Vinay Nagaraj are quality founders taking on giants in their niche--and kicking their ass. "
"
"As a bonus, Careerspan users can *actually* one click apply if they've got two stories logged!
voice_reason: Strong voice markers: assertive recommendations without hedging ('I cannot recommend... more strongly'), casual profanity ('kicking their ass'), direct enthusiasm with specificity (naming founders, referencing exact product features like 'two stories logged'). The em-dashes, italicized emphasis on *actually*, and exclamation points feel natural rather than performative. Warmth comes through substance—he's recommending specific people and a specific product feature, not just cheerleading. Loses a point because the samples are short fragments (likely LinkedIn comments) with limited room to show full rhythm and depth, but the personality signal per word is high.

text:
🚀 Skillcraft's approach is as legit as their traction "
"
"🎸 David Shacklette is a killer founder, and you won't find anyone who cares about—let alone gets—this problem as much as he does"
"
"🦾The search is powered by Careerspan—so you apply by sharing your stories and are assessed based on your qualities, not keywords"
"
"Please share this if you can with anyone who seems like a great fit!
voice_reason: Strong voice markers: 'as legit as their traction' is specific and assertive, 'killer founder' is direct without corporate gloss, 'cares about—let alone gets—this problem' uses a distinctive parenthetical escalation that shows personality. The em-dash insertion is a rhythm signature. Loses a point because the emoji-led format and 'Please share this' closing are standard LinkedIn promotional patterns, and '🚀' opener is generic social media. But the substance-first framing (traction → founder credibility → how it works → CTA) and the natural, unhyped tone clearly distinguish it from generic posts.

text:
Heads up, job seekers!"
"
"Careerspan has a job board—with a twist."
"
"Your Careerspan stories *are* your application. "
"
"And the more you share with our AI—the higher your chances of getting noticed. "
"
"Careerspan helps you turn the qualities and talent that resumes can't convey into clear hiring signals for employers. "
"
"Link in the comments!
voice_reason: Generic LinkedIn promotional post. 'Heads up, job seekers!' is stock social media opener. 'With a twist' is cliché. The exclamation points and 'Link in the comments!' are formulaic LinkedIn playbook. The core value prop sentence about 'qualities and talent that resumes can't convey' has a glimmer of V's substance-first thinking, but it's buried in a template that any social media manager could produce. No dry humor, no specificity, no assertive authority-earning.

text:
Had an absolute blast mixing it up with Emily Nelson de Velasco—including one take so spicy I had to let good sense prevail and redact it. "
"
"Not to worry though, we let plenty of opinons fly during an excellent conversation."
"
"If you haven't already checked her out, Emily is a tremendous follow—particularly for you job seekers!
voice_reason: Clear personal voice with natural warmth and energy. 'Let good sense prevail and redact it' is a distinctive, witty construction that avoids generic phrasing. 'So spicy' and 'mixing it up' show casual confidence. The direct recommendation ('tremendous follow') is assertive without hedging. Minor dings: 'absolute blast' is a common phrase, and the exclamation mark + 'Not to worry' lean slightly toward performative warmth rather than substance-driven warmth. Still a solid training signal with authentic personality markers.

text:
Quick congratulations to the folks over at Zo Computer on today's launch!"
"
"I fanboy over them hard, but stuff like this is why: https://lnkd.in/eXxDPh3j"
"
"When I first saw Aviato almost a year ago, I thought "cool product, shame I'll never use it". "
"
"Now that I use Zo, I have Apollo-grade contact enrichment I can use with natural language commands. "
"
"Btw, great work Konrad Kucharski! Your stuff lives up to the hype. "
"
"Let the networking bachmanity begin!
voice_reason: Strong voice markers: 'I fanboy over them hard' is direct and unapologetic, 'Apollo-grade contact enrichment' is specific and authoritative, and 'Let the networking bachmanity begin' shows dry humor with a Silicon Valley reference. The conversational asides ('Btw, great work') feel genuinely human. Loses a point because the LinkedIn congratulatory post format is inherently constrained—it's short and situational, so there's less room for sustained rhythm or deeper specificity. But within that format, this is distinctly V: assertive enthusiasm backed by a concrete use case, no hedging, no corporate fluff.

text:
"By show of hands, how many of you look at more than 10% of your AI meeting notes?"
"
"Out of a crowd of about 60 people, one—maybe two—hands go up."
"
"A telling illustration of the paradox of modern AI tools."
"
"They're both utterly indispensable and painfully underutilized."
"
"----"
"
"Last week, I was invited by the lovely folks over at Zo Computer to demo their product at South Park Commons and in conjunction with AI Circle."
"
"As you can see, I came in hot with the AI tooling takes. "
"
"My central claim was that modern AI tools are still being designed to fit a pre-AI information environment i.e. a "capture and store" model. "
"
"Most focus on integrating with everything and hoovering up data from everywhere."
"
"Few have a cogent philosophy around how to leverage that data for repeatable future gain. "
"
"So what's the answer? "
"
"For me, it's been building my own productivity system on Zo Computer—but for all of us?"
"
"I don't know (yet). "
"
"But I've got some interesting leads I'll be exploring here going forward: https://lnkd.in/ewRacNcb"
"
"Follow along + comment if you want access to one of the more compelling (not to mention entertaining) decks you'll read in a while!"
"
"A special thanks to Ben Guo, Rob Cheung, Albert Chun, the Zo & SPC teams, and all of my wonderful friends and supporters that made it out that night!
voice_reason: Strong voice markers throughout: opens with a concrete, theatrical anecdote (show of hands → one hand up), uses specific language ('hoovering up data', 'came in hot', 'cogent philosophy'), shows assertive framing of a contrarian take without hedging ('Most focus on... Few have...'), and the 'I don't know (yet)' is a confident admission that reads as authentic rather than weak. The parenthetical humor ('not to mention entertaining') and dry observation about the AI meeting notes paradox are distinctive. Docked from 5 because the closing CTA ('Follow along + comment if you want access') and the thank-you paragraph slip into standard LinkedIn post conventions, diluting the voice signal slightly.

text:
Not every founder optimizes for exits. Some optimize for impact."
"
"Jessica Greenwalt is in the latter mould, and she's looking for others like her!"
"
"If you're all about "moving fast and changing things", then this is your chance to get the word out."
"
"Details below 👇
voice_reason: Mostly generic LinkedIn-style promotional post. The opening line has a hint of directness but quickly falls into formulaic patterns: 'latter mould', 'this is your chance', 'Details below 👇'. Heavy on cheerleading, light on substance and specificity. No evidence-based authority, no dry humor, no distinctive rhythm. Could be written by any LinkedIn user sharing someone else's content.

text:
I always thought "builder" would remain an unfulfilled ambition."
"
"Much like "stand-up comedian" and "starting forward for Arsenal FC" (don't laugh)."
"
"Turns out I may have a lot more of that first one in me than I thought, and it's thanks to Zo Computer!"
"
"Suffice it to say, for a non-technical founder like myself, Zo has been the absolute biggest unlock. "
"
"Zo has single-handedly replaced ChatGPT, Notion, n8n, and like 3 other products for me."
"
"And if you're even marginally tech-y, it could be a gamechanger for you as well. "
"
"That's why I'm chuffed to be speaking at their Builder Happy Hour with AI Circle at South Park Commons on Nov 5th. "
"
"You'll hear the sagely Ben Guo drop vibe coding wisdom for the ages, and I'll cap off the talk by demoing some of the cooler use cases I've built out. "
"
"Don't miss out. Event link is in the comments.
voice_reason: Highly characteristic. Self-deprecating humor with specificity (Arsenal FC, stand-up comedian), assertive product endorsement without corporate fluff ('single-handedly replaced'), British-inflected vocabulary ('chuffed', 'sagely'), concrete details (named products, named person, specific event), warmth through substance (lists exact tools replaced rather than vague praise). The parenthetical '(don't laugh)' and 'like 3 other products' show natural conversational rhythm. This couldn't be written by a generic copywriter.

text:
I need a volunteer!"
"
"Are you someone who —"
"1) Loves the intersection of AI and productivity?"
"2) Has 45-60m to spare this weekend to help me field test a side project I'm deploying?"
"
"If so, please drop a comment or DM."
"
"As for what's in it for you?"
"
"1) Pre-launch access to the *actual* most powerful AI product of 2025 (yes—I said it)"
"2) A semi-custom productivity suite (~$500)—for free"
"
"I'm not normally so forward, but believe me when I say this—I've got something incredible to show you. "
"
"#wearezoback
voice_reason: Has some authentic energy and the bold claim style ('actual most powerful AI product of 2025') fits V's assertive-without-hedging trait. But the structure is formulaic LinkedIn engagement bait—numbered lists, 'drop a comment or DM,' the false modesty of 'I'm not normally so forward.' The hashtag and overall cadence feel more like growth-hacking template than distinctive voice. Flashes of personality ('yes—I said it') but not enough specificity or substance to be a strong training signal.

text:
Been thinking a lot about why most career tools fall flat...."
"
"IMO: they presuppose we already know ourselves well enough to use it effectively."
"
"So let me be the first to say to them: we don't, and that's the real problem. "
"
"That's why this October 28, I'm joining Eric Stano from Magic EdTech and Lisa Noble from Colby College's Halloran Lab for a conversation about how lifelong learning is reshaping what career growth actually looks like. "
"
"On this occasion, we'll have the pleasure of having hot takes cajoled out of us by Magic Edtech's wonderful Olivia Lara-Gresty."
"
"Whether you're an HR/L&D professional curious about what to look out for in careertech vendors or just a job seeker with more doubts than answers, you'll find something worth taking away from this panel. "
"
"Registration in the comments! See you there.
voice_reason: Strong opening hook with a specific, direct claim ('most career tools fall flat'). The 'IMO' + assertive follow-up is characteristic — states a position without hedging. 'hot takes cajoled out of us' and the warm shout-out to Olivia show dry humor and warmth-through-substance. 'more doubts than answers' is a nice human touch that avoids corporate speak. Docked from 5 because the middle section drifts into standard event-promo language ('we'll have the pleasure of,' 'whether you're an HR/L&D professional') and the CTA is generic LinkedIn boilerplate. The voice is clearly present in the bookends but diluted in the promotional core.

text:
Aki writes consistently thoughtful pieces on the candidate experience. "
"
"I encourage y'all to approach her and share your job hunting war stories. "
"
"Having spoken to so many of you, I know they are worth sharing -- both as guidance and as succor for others.
voice_reason: Distinctive voice markers: 'y'all' (regional/personal warmth), 'war stories' (vivid metaphor choice), 'sucor' (deliberately elevated vocabulary showing range), double-dash em-dash style. The 'Having spoken to so many of you' earns authority through experience rather than claiming it. Warm but not saccharine — substance-driven encouragement. Loses a point because it's relatively short and the structure is somewhat standard endorsement format, but the language choices are clearly personal.

text:
If I've said it once, I've said it a thousand times... "
"
"👏 Energy and confidence management are the absolute most underrated part of job hunting"
"
"Look: you are not a machine for turning hopes and dreams into ATS-compliant resumes. "
"
"🫂 You are a person participating in a draining process that certainly won't let you rest if you don't give yourself some grace. "
"
"I know that's not easy -- I've been in that place of alternating between maniacally applying for jobs and dreading even opening my laptop as my self-esteem ticks lower with each unrequited workday application. "
"
"👌 That's why I love this post from Kim Wilkes."
"
"Not *just* because of the Careerspan signal boost (which is always appreciated!)"
"
"👉 But because she's sharing a powerful reminder that you'll go further and feel better if you punctuate your job search with activities that are grounding and rejuvenating.
voice_reason: Packed with voice markers: the direct 'Look:' address, the vivid specific image of 'maniacally applying for jobs and dreading even opening my laptop,' the self-deprecating vulnerability that earns authority through lived experience, the dry parenthetical '(which is always appreciated!),' and the assertive framing ('absolute most underrated') without corporate hedging. The rhythm alternates between punchy short sentences and longer confessional ones. 'ATS-compliant resumes' and 'unrequited workday application' are distinctive, specific word choices that couldn't come from a template. Strong warmth-through-substance throughout.

text:
David Speigel 🔮 has forgotten more about networking than most people have learned in their whole lives. "
"
"If you're job searching or just looking to network better, run—don't walk—to this free session.
voice_reason: The 'forgotten more than most people have learned' construction is a well-worn idiom, not original language. 'Run—don't walk' is similarly cliché. There's warmth and directness, and the endorsement is specific to a person, but the phrasing relies on stock expressions rather than distinctive voice. It reads like a competent social media promotion, not a voice-rich sample.

text:
What does it mean to be "happily employed"?"
"
"Different things to different people, no doubt."
"
"But with $400B lost *annually* due to hiring churn and up to 80% of folks looking to change jobs in a given year..."
"
"A lot of us are clearly trying to figure that out for ourselves. "
"
"That's why Careerspan is proud to be partnering with Ainsley Fagerström and the Happily Employed community."
"
"HE members are going to receive the *most tailored job recommendations*—bar none."
"
"Jobs that are:"
"👉 Aligned with their goals, hard skills, and soft skills -- not just resume keywords"
"🚨 Absent any dealbreakers they tell our AI "
"✅ Free of red flags courtesy of our BS detector"
"📍 Location aware (but with plenty of remote options)"
"🎯 On company’s portal, not to some random job board"
"📬 Posted in under 48 hours from initial posting"
"⭐️️ Delivered with strategic positioning guidance + one-click resume tailoring"
"
"And that's after setting aside the fact that, by using Careerspan, they're going to be better prepared than ever!"
"
"So check out Happily Employed powered by Careerspan, and join the 20% of folks who already have their dream job!
voice_reason: Strong voice markers throughout: the rhetorical opener, the '$400B lost *annually*' specificity-as-authority move, 'BS detector' as dry humor, and the list format with punchy descriptors ('bar none', 'not just resume keywords'). The emoji-list structure is a LinkedIn convention but V makes it his own with assertive language ('Free of red flags', 'not to some random job board'). Minor dings: the closing line ('join the 20%') feels slightly generic-promotional, and 'proud to be partnering' edges toward corporate speak he'd normally avoid. But overall this reads as distinctly V—direct, evidence-led, warmth earned through substance rather than sentiment.

text:
Question for the active and passive job seekers on LinkedIn -- would you *pay* for personalized job recommendations delivered to your inbox regularly?"
"
"When I say personalized, I mean *truly personalized*:"
"👉 Aligned with your goals, hard skills, and soft skills"
"👉 Factors in your dealbreakers + knows when you lack a hard requirement"
"👉 Location aware (but with plenty of remote options) "
"👉 Only routes you to the company's portal, not to some random job board"
"👉 Delivered in under 48 hours from initial posting"
"
"As a bonus:"
"⭐️ Filtered through our BS detector so you don't see any scams "
"⭐️ Shows you gaps in your profile before you apply to maximize your shot
voice_reason: Strong voice signal. Direct question opening with no preamble, specific feature list that earns authority through concrete details (48-hour delivery, company portal routing), and dry humor landing well ('BS detector'). The italicized *truly personalized* is a distinctive emphasis pattern showing assertiveness. Minor deductions: the emoji-heavy LinkedIn format is somewhat conventional for the platform, and 'maximize your shot' leans slightly generic. But the substance-first structure — here's the question, here's exactly what I mean, here's the bonus — is characteristically V: warmth delivered through specificity rather than sentiment.

text:
Them: ChatGPT is such a suck-up. "
"
"Me, who got carried away with personalization: *through tears* yeah, totally, also can I get a hug?
voice_reason: Strong self-deprecating humor with sharp observational irony — criticizing AI sycophancy while admitting emotional dependency. The '*through tears*' stage direction and the whiplash pivot from agreement to vulnerability is a distinctive comedic move. Good voice signal: specific, human, no hedging on the joke. Docked from 5 only because the meme-dialogue format is common on social media, so the container is generic even if the content is personal.

text:
Thanks, Tony Case, for shedding light on the procedural nightmare of applying for jobs in modern day. "
"
"Having to retype the same details about your background each time you apply—most of which can be found in the resume, mind you—is a type of Sisyphean hell I wouldn't wish on anyone."
"
"Who else thinks we need a Common App for job hunters?
voice_reason: Shows some personality with 'Sisyphean hell' and the parenthetical 'mind you,' but the structure is a standard LinkedIn engagement post (thanks @person, restate their point, rhetorical question to audience). The 'Who else thinks' closer is a generic engagement-bait pattern. The observation itself is relatable but not particularly specific or evidence-backed — it's a common complaint stated competently rather than a distinctive take. Moderate voice signal diluted by formulaic social media framing.

text:
I've been wondering something. "
"
"Would especially love thoughts from folks that hire (founders, TA, hiring managers, etc.)
voice_reason: Fragment with almost no content. 'I've been wondering something' is maximally vague—the opposite of V's specificity. 'Would especially love thoughts from folks that hire' is generic LinkedIn engagement-bait phrasing. No substance, no evidence, no distinctive language. Zero training signal.

text:
🚨 Here's a fact: Your dream hire is probably terrible at applying for jobs."
"
"Think about it."
"
"That 10x engineer? Been coding for 5 years straight. Last updated their resume in 2019."
"
"That unicorn marketer? Too busy tripling revenue to practice interview answers."
"
"Your perfect candidate is crushing it at work—not polishing their LinkedIn weekly."
"
"Unfortunately, self-advocacy is a muscle. Use it or lose it."
"
"And when you're genuinely good at your job, you don't exercise that muscle. "
"
"You're too busy... being good at your job."
"
"Yet somehow, we've created a hiring system that rewards job-search practice over job performance."
"
"So how do we fix this?"
"
"The SAT gives everyone free prep materials. "
"Not to make it easier, but to make it fair. "
"To measure what actually matters--relative performance on equal footing. "
"
"🤔 Why don't we do this for hiring?"
"
"Why don't we help people put forward their best selves?"
"
"This isn't about charity—it's about meritocracy."
"
"When every candidate can articulate their value clearly, you finally get to compare apples to apples. "
"
"Real capability vs. real capability. "
"Not "who hired a resume writer" vs. "who didn't."
"
"At Careerspan, we help candidates uncover their real stories through AI-guided reflection. "
"No BS templates. "
"Just structured conversations that reveal who they actually are."
"
"👉 Because when you help candidates shine, you can see who shines brightest."
"
"And right now? "
"You're not rejecting bad candidates. "
"🤦 You're rejecting bad self-advocates that might be your next superstar hire. "
"
"If you're ready to fix this for your organization, let's connect!
voice_reason: Strong voice signal. The rhetorical structure (setup → insight → solution) is distinctive and confident. Good specificity ('10x engineer coding for 5 years,' 'resume from 2019,' SAT analogy). Assertive framing without hedging ('Here's a fact,' 'This isn't about charity'). The dry humor lands ('Too busy... being good at your job'). Docked from 5 because: the emoji usage (🚨🤔🤦👉) and LinkedIn-post cadence (one-liner paragraphs, dramatic spacing) push it toward a recognizable social media formula rather than a fully unique rhythm. The CTA at the end ('let's connect!') is standard LinkedIn. But the core argument—self-advocacy as a muscle, hiring rewarding practice over performance—is specific, evidence-grounded, and clearly V's domain expertise showing through. Good training signal for voice, moderate noise from platform conventions.

text:
Networking is *literally* the CEO's job."
"
"And by that measure, for over a year, I was the 0.1x founder"
"
"Too busy with product." "Locked down with fundraising." "Building the team comes first."
"All true. All excuses."
"
"The real reason?"
"I didn't feel worthy of those rooms."
"Didn't feel worthy of introducing myself as a CEO."
"Didn't feel worthy of taking up space in conversations that mattered."
"
"I said what everyone says: "Networking feels so transactional."
"What I really meant was: "I don't feel worthy of anyone's time."
"
"Then, a few months ago, I forced myself to a (fantastic) breakfast organized by Andrew and the Fibe team."
"
"It was the best decision I've made in some time. "
"
"I was worried that it would be the 0.1x founder that showed up. "
"
"Instead, I threw myself into the deep end and found:"
"👉 Conversations that actually mattered."
"🤝 A partnership that re-energized our business. "
"👊 Connections that became real friends. "
"
"The difference?"
"
"After a year of building Careerspan, I finally knew our story."
"Not just knew it."
"I'd dissected it. Analyzed it. Internalized every bit of it."
"
"I could explain our vision in my sleep. "
"Our mission had become muscle memory. "
"Our impact was measured by an outpouring of user love. "
"
"Suddenly, talking about it was as easy as breathing."
"Because I wasn't selling."
"I was just... sharing what I knew to be true."
"
"So here's the lesson:"
"Your story matters."
"But first, you need to understand it so deeply that telling it comes naturally. "
"
"That's why we built Careerspan."
"🪏 To help you excavate your story. "
"📐 To understand it from every angle. "
"🧘‍♂️ To make sharing it as easy as breathing.
voice_reason: Strong voice throughout. The '0.1x founder' coinage is distinctive and specific. Vulnerability is delivered without self-pity—direct, assertive admissions ('All true. All excuses.'). The reframe from 'networking feels transactional' to 'I don't feel worthy' is sharp observational honesty. Rhythm is intentional—short punchy lines building to longer resolutions. Earns authority through lived specificity (naming Andrew, Fibe, the timeline). The pivot to Careerspan at the end is natural, not salesy—warmth-through-substance. Rich training signal for voice fine-tuning.

text:
Thank you to all of our new followers for helping me lose the hell outta this bet. "
"
"Sadly, no more Fancy Feasts for my boys. We're a Whisker's household for the rest of August. "
"
"Logan Currie - Double or nothing to 3000 *by September 1*?
voice_reason: Highly distinctive voice. The playful specificity (Fancy Feasts vs Whisker's, 'for the rest of August'), the direct call-out by name with a competitive escalation, dry humor about losing a bet framed as gratitude, and the casual warmth ('my boys') all create a unique, unmistakable personality. No hedging, no corporate speak — just confident, funny, human writing with real details that only this person would write.

text:
🚩 Let's call it what it is -- there's a lot of BS jobs out there."
"
"Titles like "Chief Happiness Engineer" and "Digital Transformation Sherpa"
"
"Spin like "thrives in ambiguity" when the rest of the JD screams "we don't know what you'll be doing!"
"
"And the classic "competitive salary". Competitive with what? The poverty line?"
"
"Whether intentional or not, you should be aware of red flags in any job to which you apply. "
"
"That's why we built a BS Job Detector. "
"
"Just drop in the job description, and we'll highlight:"
"⚫️ Potential role misrepresentations"
"⚫️ Cultural red flags"
"⚫️ Unrealistic expectations "
"⚫️ Compensation Concerns"
"⚫️ Key clarifying questions to ask during interviews"
"
"It's totally free - no login or credit card needed. "
"
"Because if your dream job is actually a nightmare, it's best to know before you even apply!"
"
"🔗 Link in the comments. "
"
"Please share and repost for visibility and help out your peers who are job seeking!
voice_reason: Strong voice signal. The opening is direct and assertive ('Let's call it what it is'), the humor is dry and specific ('Competitive with what? The poverty line?'), and the examples are concrete rather than abstract. The tone is professional but human — no corporate speak. Minor deductions: the closing CTA ('Please share and repost for visibility') is generic LinkedIn boilerplate, and 'dream job is actually a nightmare' leans cliché. But the overall piece has clear personality markers, confident rhythm, and warmth-through-substance — V is earning authority by naming specific BS patterns rather than just claiming expertise.

text:
The weapons-grade intensity of my smugness may rip us apart as cofounders."
"
"I beg of you."
"
"If you want Careerspan to survive, please don't let me win."
"
"I will be physically incapable of not pushing Logan Currie's buttons if she loses.
voice_reason: Unmistakably personal. 'Weapons-grade intensity of my smugness' is a signature-level phrase — specific, self-aware, theatrically escalated. The dramatic framing of a petty interpersonal dynamic ('if you want Careerspan to survive') as existential stakes is textbook dry humor. Names a real person, names the company, uses double-negative emphasis ('physically incapable of not pushing buttons') — every line drips with a voice that could not be anyone else's.

text:
Humans use AI to spam applications."
"Companies use AI to filter out spam."
"
"It's a situation so tragic, Greek playwrights would call this "a bit much"
"
"So let me break it down Barney style for y'all. "
"
"Auto-apply is like me on the dance floor: it has a *very* limited set of moves and by jove is it going to use all of them."
"
"Swap "managed" for "spearheaded."
"Guess that you "increased engagement by 25%." (why is it always 25%?)"
"Make up something so generic, the pharma industry wants to ban it"
"
"That's it. That's their whole schtick."
"
"It's as futile as trying to elevate a Ramen dinner."
"You can change the flavor packet."
"Add some frozen vegetables."
"Throw in some cheese if you're a nutcase like me."
"But when it's all said and done, that's still ramen."
"
"Because they don't really know you. And they don't care to as long as you'll pay them $50 a month and feel good that "number go up"
"
"Meanwhile, your *actual* career?"
"That's a walk-in fridge full of prime ingredients."
"The time you saved a product launch from disaster."
"Building a following from 500 to 5,000 during a brand crisis."
"The junior colleagues you mentored who all got promoted."
"
"Real stories. Real numbers. Real impact."
"
"Valuable context that auto-apply can't do jacksh*t without. "
"
"It's making instant ramen while your Michelin-worthy ingredients sit in the back--untouched, forgotten, and about to rot anytime. "
"
"Instead, it's the same, sad noodles hundreds of times a day."
"And hoping the hiring manager happens to be really, really hungry."
"
"So do yourself a massive favor: take stock of your career inventory."
"
"Because then, you'll really be cooking with gas!
voice_reason: Textbook V voice. Extended metaphor (ramen vs. Michelin ingredients) is sustained with escalating specificity and humor. Self-deprecating asides ('if you're a nutcase like me', 'me on the dance floor'), dry observational wit ('why is it always 25%?', 'Greek playwrights would call this a bit much'), and assertive directness ('can't do jacksh*t without') are all strong personality markers. The rhythm alternates between punchy fragments ('That's it. That's their whole schtick.') and flowing elaboration. Warmth-through-substance is clear: the humor serves a real argument about career value vs. automation theater. Zero corporate speak, zero hedging. Highly distinctive training signal.

text:
Got another fascinating job recommendation today: Music Central Assistant at Endeavor."
"
"Their algorithm's logic? Flawless."
"
"I exist in New York. They exist in New York. I was once in a team. They have a team."
"
"My decade in career coaching? Irrelevant."
"

"4. Even with guardrails, there's no harm in double-checking your output on mission-critical tasks "

"I just read a fascinating piece (link in the comments) by Michael Horn and Bob Moesta that perfectly captures what I've been seeing in the job market - we're trapped in a vicious cycle where AI tools are making things WORSE, not better."
"
"Here's the toxic pattern they've identified:"
"• Job seekers use AI to blast out hundreds of generic applications"
"• Employers get flooded with thousands of AI-generated resumes"
"• Companies either double-down on their own AI screening or abandon it entirely"
"• Hiring managers retreat to the safety of personal referrals"
"• Qualified candidates without connections get left behind"
"
"It's essentially mutually assured destruction for both sides of the hiring equation."
"
"In my opinion, the current generation of candidate-side AI tools are primarily fueling this problem because they focus on brute-force volume rather than meaningful personalization. "
"
"LazyApply, JobCopilot and their ilk are selling false hope - tricking job seekers into thinking they're making progress when they're actually just contributing to the noise."
"
"But here's where I think Careertech founders need to listen up: We need AI tools with INTEGRITY. Not tools that help you spam 500 employers, but tools that help you create genuinely tailored, authentic applications for roles you're actually qualified for."
"
"Until then, I'm with Horn and Moesta - invest your time in building real relationships instead of feeding the application machine."
"
"#AIHiring #JobSearch #NetworkingMatters #CareerTech

"It is by definition constrained by what it knows (i. e. What you tell it). "

"
"So when you ask it to generate a cover letter or come up with new resume bullets, your stuff will come out sounding the same as everyone else's. "
"
"This problem is made worse when people give ChatGPT basic instructions that don't tell it what information to prioritize and or what structure to use, so it once again resorts to its base understanding of what to do. "

text:
"Whatever value they provide for weary job seekers, it’s worth noting that spray-and-pray [AI tools] don’t address the larger issue that prevents many applicants from landing a job” "
"
"Aki Ito really got to the heart of the matter in their Business Insider article on AI Job Search Bots: there’s an applicant arms race going on, where people are applying to more jobs than ever - and by all accounts, hearing back less and less. "
"
"Employers have had an overabundance of candidates long before these spray-and-pray tools emerged - there were an average of 250 applicants per corporate opening in 2023. I wouldn’t be surprised to see that number double in 2024."
"
"So sure, you could splurge on one of these tools, but if you can’t effectively communicate how great of a candidate you are in the first place, it won’t matter how many places you apply to: you’ll be invisible to employers who can’t see the best “you”. "
"
"I call this the “self-advocacy skill gap”.  Basically, how good is someone at putting themselves forward as a great candidate? This can be through their materials, their networking outreach messages, or interview answers. "
"
"In my experience, people are, at most, okay at self-advocacy – they can’t translate the great things about themselves into messaging that’s appealing and accessible to potential employers. The result is a resume or outreach message that looks like it’s coming from only a “just ok” candidate.  And in today’s job market, “just ok” is far from good enough. "
"
"So for those of you struggling to hear back about interviews or make it past the screening call, ask yourselves this: is the version of myself I’m putting out there - in terms of my materials or interview answers - top quartile quality? "
"
"If not, there isn’t a job search bot in the world that can help you until you lock down your messaging and get good at putting it forward."
"
"Luckily, we’re building a tool to help people close their self-advocacy skill gap at The ApplyAI. It’s free for another few months while we test new features, so sign up at www.theapply.ai and check it out. "
"
"P.S. Leave a comment or DM me if you want to be personally onboarded to the tool. I’ll show you exactly how to use our tool to take your job search/career growth journey to the next level."
"
"Like I said, it's a free tool, so I can't make it a sales pitch even if I tired!"
"
"Link to Aki's article: https://lnkd.in/grtkaN9W
voice_reason: Strong voice markers throughout: coined terminology ('self-advocacy skill gap'), direct assertions without hedging ('I wouldn't be surprised'), specific evidence (250 applicants stat), conversational-but-authoritative tone. But the core argument structure — external citation → reframing the problem → naming it → actionable challenge to the reader — is distinctly V's pattern.

text:
Ever felt your dream job was completely out of reach? "
"Ever felt completely lost while preparing for it? "
"Ever thought your prep wouldn’t make a difference? "

"At the start of my second year of the MBA, I felt all that and more when I decided to apply to McKinsey. I was totally out of my depth because I hadn’t recruited for consulting the previous year, so there was a lot of catching up to do and not much time to do it. "
"
"So I did the only thing I could: I put in dozens of hours to learn everything I could about casing and their interview process and catalog it in painful detail. The byproduct was an only *slightly* intense set of notes that I studied religiously. "
"
"Spoiler alert: I got the job! "
"
"What’s more, those notes that I had shared casually with some classmates became a big hit: they’ve racked up 1600+ views since September 2021 (see screenshot). That’s a lot considering Johnson only has about 200 people applying for consulting jobs across both years. "
"
"In retrospect, it makes total sense why I ended up enjoying getting through that interview process - it’s the same reason why I started The ApplyAI! Getting a great job is far from straightforward, and my team and I are planning to change that. "
"
"That’s why I’m here to share that same resource with LinkedIn. Here’s the McKinsey version (http://bit.ly/3NX5wST) and here’s a Firm-agnostic version (https://bit.ly/48rAbA1) for people looking at places besides the Firm. Please note that these are *supplementary* resources; they aren’t intended to build your capabilities from scratch. Think of them as an additional perspective on casing or behavioral interviews."
"
"If you were one of the people these notes helped, I’d love for you to leave a comment or repost for visibility! "
"
"For everyone else, follow us at The ApplyAI, sign up for our beta waitlist at www.theapply.ai, and watch this space for the even more exciting announcement that’s coming soon. My co-founder Logan and I think that’s wild that job hunters, particularly fresh grads, are expected to just “figure it out” without real help. We’re here to change that. "
"
"Finally, a huge thanks to my peers at Johnson who helped me get one of the biggest opportunities of my life. Here's a non-exhaustive list: Andrew Shin Sebastian Rodriguez Goumashvili Kevin Wang Madhuri Mehta Jacob Lehman Surina Shahani Uma Rajeswari Somireddi Thomas Stevenson Tarun Johnson Deepanshu Mehta José Enrique Gutiérrez Montaño. The camaraderie is what makes our program special, and I’m so grateful to have had the firsthand experience.

A brief update for my wonderful peers and colleagues on LinkedIn:"

"⏲️ What I’ve been up to"

So, I did what any normal person would do: I prepared a 1000-word memo (read: screed) that sketched out the fundamentals of what we're building today. text:
Using AI for the job search is an exercise in using it correctly -- and sparingly.

Ideally, it's used to help aggregate opportunities (e.g. Warmer Jobs) and improve the underlying ability of a candidate to advocate for themselves, particularly early career folks (aka what we do at Careerspan).

Conversely, stay far away from auto apply tools (which are glorified spam bots that'll have you applying for both a gardening position in the midwest and an investment banking internship in New York City -- and that's if your name doesn't get flagged in the ATS for submitting junk apps). 

Most resume generation tools are garbage as well because they don't make an effort to get to know the individual first, so anything they produce is necessarily generic or fabricated. That's why folks are better off just using ChatGPT because at least it has memory. 

Hope that helps!
voice_reason: Textbook V voice. Direct stance ('stay far away'), specific examples (gardening position in midwest vs IB internship in NYC), dry humor ('glorified spam bots'), authority earned through domain knowledge not posturing, warmth without softness ('Hope that helps!' feels genuine not performative). The parenthetical asides are distinctive rhythm markers. 'Most resume generation tools are garbage' — assertive, no hedging. Natural Careerspan plug woven into substance, not salesy. Strong training signal.

text:

text:
When I was interning at Gainsight, I took on an effort to figure out why tool adoption among sellers was low. Surveyed maybe 30 or so of the ~80 sellers across all major geographies and product lines. 

A couple of observations:
- Generally, people don't want to learn new tools — even if it makes their life better or easier. 
- Lots of companies suffer from "if we buy it, they will come" syndrome; a lot of thought needs to be put into building the desire—in both individuals and the collective—to use the tool. 
- Having "power users" is key — power users find joy in learning about a product and often have a desire to share that with others. No enablement strategy is better than a really hyped employee recording a loom explaining why tool XYZ will change their lives (coming from the boss doesn't count for the same reason why listening to our parents' advice is harder than listening to a friend's)
voice_reason: Highly characteristic. Specific details (30 of ~80 sellers, Gainsight internship) earn authority. Parenthetical humor about bosses vs parents is dry and observational. 'If we buy it, they will come' syndrome shows original framing. No hedging — states observations directly. The casual precision ('maybe 30 or so') feels human without being sloppy. Bullet structure with escalating insight mirrors a mind that organizes naturally. Strong training signal.

text:
Gokul Rajaram - Tl;Dr: Hard skills (which credentials relate to) are table stakes. We actually need signal into the subjective side of candidates.

We want to hire for qualities, but the current paradigm/technology only allows us to scan for keywords and thus over-rely on proxies. A resume can't *really* tell me if someone's hardworking, so I use the fact that they graduated from an Ivy as a proxy and hope I can validate that in the interview.

News flash: they can't. 46% of hires fail by the 18-month mark. 89% of the reason they fail is a values, mindset, and soft-skills mismatch. ONly 11% was because of their hard skills, which is what credentials are relevant to.

What we need is more high-fidelity candidate signal into their behavioral and psychological side (attained in a scalable way) that allows us to filter based on who someone is, not what credentials they carry.

Brian Bies I appreciate your support. For me, the following elements tend to be key:
1) Prioritized search - start with existing connections that you can easily re-initiate contact with, move on to 2nd degree connections for whom you can receive a warm introduction, and  then turn to cold outreach if necessary. Following this pattern tends to be useful even if you ultimate need cold outreach because it gives you a lot of great practice before the real challenge
2) Personalize outreach - this boils down to finding points of commonality with someone. The more recent the better. Use this as a wedge to grab their attention. More importantly, no one likes a generic message that they think you've sent to a dozen people before

text:
Don't Get Spooked by Ghost Jobs: A Smarter Approach to Your Job Search

Don't Get Spooked by Ghost Jobs: A Smarter Approach to Your Job Search

Created on 2025-04-22 12:58

Published on 2025-04-22 13:02

If you've been job hunting lately, you've probably experienced this: You spend hours tailoring your resume and crafting the perfect cover letter for what looks like your dream role. Days pass. Weeks pass. Nothing but crickets.

It's not just you. You've likely encountered what we in the industry call a "ghost job" - a position advertised with zero intent to hire. According to recent data from Greenhouse, these phantom postings make up a staggering 18-22% of all job listings. The Wall Street Journal confirms this troubling trend, noting that roughly 1 in 5 online job ads may be ghost jobs.

So why do companies post jobs they have no intention of filling? Sometimes they're building a candidate pipeline for future openings. Other times, they're signaling growth to investors or employees. Some are holding out for that mythical "unicorn" candidate, while others are simply complying with public-posting requirements after making an internal hire.

Whatever the reason, the impact on job seekers is real. Three in five candidates suspect they've encountered ghost jobs, leading to wasted time, diminished confidence, and increasingly desperate tactics.

How Should You Respond?

Rather than giving up or resorting to the mass-application approach (which only dilutes your personal brand), here's how to navigate this haunted job market:

Adopt a tiered effort strategy. Not all job postings deserve the same level of attention. Save your energy for high-alignment roles while using a more streamlined approach for everything else.

When you find a role that truly excites you, go all in. This means a tailored resume, customized cover letter, and strategic networking outreach, all submitted as quickly as possible after the job is posted.

Learn to spot the ghosts. Check how long the posting has been active, whether it has a clear timestamp, and if it appears on the company's own website. Favor platforms that verify their job listings.

Speed + personalization is the winning formula. When you do find a legitimate opportunity, being both fast and distinctive gives you the best chance of standing out.

Coming Soon: Tools to Combat the Ghost Job Epidemic

At Careerspan, we've been working on a solution to help you navigate this challenging landscape, set to launch next month.

Our new JD Gut Check will evaluate JDs based on your true alignment -- not just based on keyword or hard skills basis, but also your soft-skills alignment, culture fit, etc., helping you prioritize where to invest your valuable time.

For those promising opportunities that pass the authenticity check, our Smart Application Suite will generate professionally tailored materials in minutes. This means you can submit high-quality, personalized applications quickly while maintaining the unique voice that makes you stand out from the crowd.

The Job Search Shouldn't Feel Like a Maze

The real edge in today's job market isn't volume—it's precision and speed. Knowing which roles are genuine and hitting them hard with materials that resonate will serve you better than scattering generic applications across the internet.

Stop wasting precious time on positions that don't actually exist. The job market is challenging enough without chasing phantoms. With the right strategy—and soon, the right tools—you can focus your energy where it matters most.

Stay tuned for our launch next month. Your job search is about to get a whole lot more effective!
voice_reason: Reads like generic LinkedIn content marketing. Heavy on formulaic structures ('here's how to...', 'this means...'), corporate-friendly hedging ('challenging landscape', 'valuable time'), and listicle cadence. The 'ghost job' framing is mildly clever but the execution is safe and explanatory rather than direct or observational. No dry humor, no sharp specificity, no distinctive rhythm. The Careerspan plug at the end is standard startup blog fare. Could have been written by any content marketer — very little of V's assertive, evidence-through-substance voice comes through.

text:
Out with the Old, in with the Niche

Out with the Old, in with the Niche

Created on 2020-04-17 12:20

Published on 2020-04-17 12:36

Change is the only constant on the internet, and this time, it’s coming for today’s social media behemoths. The once unquestionable dominance of companies like Facebook and Twitter has already begun to fade. For years now, they have been bleeding fringe users and struggling to acquire new ones. 

Millennials and Gen Zers are especially wary. They are far savvier online, more concerned by issues like privacy, and generally inclined to support companies that operate ethically. Instead, such users are increasingly turning to platforms whose communities are oriented around a particular interest, goal, or principle. I believe that this shift marks a new era in social networking—a growing rejection of the status quo and embracing of crowdculture. 

Currently, big players like Facebook are pursuing an expansive growth strategy focused on providing a broad array of services and features. Facebook, in particular, has made an effort to position itself as a one-stop-shop, not only for one’s social media needs but also for “IRL” purposes such as payment. This generalist nature and broad appeal, however, has its downsides: it does many things, but only a few them especially well. 

This overabundance of features, many of which do not operate effectively on mobile devices, has led to a convoluted platform that can charitably be described as a mess. By contrast, niche networks have taken a different route by offering specialized and well-defined value propositions. The evidence would suggest that this approach appeals to younger users, who are already used to splitting their engagement over several services. 

We need only look at the tremendous range of niche social networks to understand their appeal and explosive growth. There are established names like Pinterest and Tinder and newcomers like Tapped, a geosocial networking service for beer lovers, and Houzz, a social website about home decorating ideas. These communities are oriented around singular purposes—be it a profession or industry, hobby, or even shared experience—and designed to foster deeper connections. By contrast, on the big social sites, a person’s network is often so big that there’s no real sense of community. 

These networks also benefit from a clean slate as compared to Facebook and Twitter. These tech giants are regularly in the news for all the wrong reasons—from privacy violations to the treatment of their staff. Younger users care about whether the company they subscribe to is “problematic.” They are also more likely to seek out alternatives to mainstream brands with stronger community-oriented practices. For instance, despite weathering criticism over the years, Reddit has generally been responsive to the needs of its users—even if its response wasn’t to everyone’s satisfaction. 

Perhaps the ultimate proof is that these Goliaths of social networking are running scared is the fact that they’re very much hedging their bets. Facebook, for instance, made a conscious decision to limit integration between its primary brand and subsidiaries like Whatsapp and Instagram. As a result, they have avoided the dilution of their unique selling points and negative associations with their parent company. Indeed, these multi-billion- dollar brands are evidence of the tremendous growth potential of niche social networks. 

In many ways, these niche social networks reflect a change in what we want out of our online lives. Most of us will probably remain on mainstream platforms for the foreseeable future, but I believe our usage will increasingly reflect who we are and how we define ourselves. It’ll skew towards intimate networks that enable its users to share experiences and ideas in an authentic and personal way. Our various online presences will mirror who we are on the inside, not just how we want to present ourselves on the outside. You may not be able to do everything on niche social networks, but you’ll undoubtedly get what you came for.

text:
The Auto-Applycolypse Will Doom Us All

The Auto-Applycolypse Will Doom Us All

Created on 2025-06-24 15:25

Published on 2025-06-24 15:45

Auto-apply this, auto-apply that....why don’t you stop auto-f*cking your chances of getting a job by using terrible AI tools that don’t have your best interests at heart. 

Don’t take it from me – The NYT thinks the same (article in the comments), as do the dozens of Chief HR Officers and Talent leaders I’ve spoken to in the last two months. 

All auto-apply tools offer is the false comfort of "number go up". They exploit your desperation with vanity metrics. "Congrats! You've applied to 847 jobs this week!". 

What these companies aren’t telling you - because they know you won’t check - is that their AI agents are just as likely to put in an app for a project manager at Meta as they are forklift operator in Fargo. 

They rely on a well-known bias call "metric fixation" and boy do they know you'll fixate. 

Never mind that you're getting a 0.2% response rate - because quantity is obviously ≠ quality - and then internalizing that as you sucking when it’s just your approach that sucks. 

Auto-apply companies know this - and they literally don't care. 

You’re playing the wrong “numbers game”

“But V - it’s a numbers game…right?”, you ask uncomfortably as the realization that you were just repeating a meaningless cliche dawns on you. 

Well, yes and no.

Yes - the number of shots on goal generally correlates with your likelihood to score EXCEPT if all you’re doing is yeeting the ball in any given direction 

A 2023 academic study (Campero & Kacperczyk) involving over 140,000 applications found that even minor tailoring (adjusting job titles or keywords) produced measurable improvements in interview rates—outperforming even the effect of internal referrals. Our own internal study found that semantically tailored resumes averaged an ATS score lift of 18% over generic resumes. 

You’re not sticking it to the system, you’re gumming up the works

Also, and I hate to be that guy, but you (yes, you specifically) using auto-apply is making it worse for EVERYONE

Every spammed application gives employers another excuse to raise barriers. To add more hoops. To retreat further behind referral walls. Heck, 70% of jobs never even end up on a public job board—they're filled through the "hidden job market" of connections and word-of-mouth, now more than ever. 

One last thing: Employers, you brought this upon yourselves 

You spent decades filtering candidates through tired signals—prestigious degrees, brand-name employers, keyword bingo—rather than seeking deeper understanding of the humans worth considering. 

You set up a candidate evaluation paradigm that that is superficial at best and outright inequitable at worst because “there’s plenty of fish in the sea” – why get better at picking when you can just be pickier. 

You automated away humanity, then made the surprised pikachu face when candidates fight fire with fire. 

Don’t expect sympathy from candidates - instead, I advise you to get better at selecting for human traits and not the presence of keywords.  

So where do go from here? Here’s my two cents. Candidates - Tell authentic stories. Make real connections. Don’t come off as a bot; show up as a real person. And for the love of all that is holy, stop spamming your resume. Because auto-apply isn't your secret weapon—it's career self-sabotage wrapped in Silicon Valley marketing.

Employers - take a long, hard look at your hiring process. Find ways to separate the wheat from the chaff on a more nuanced, human-centric basis. 

Because avoiding mutually assured exasperation will require both sides to de-escalate.

text:
When I was interning at Gainsight, I took on an effort to figure out why tool adoption among sellers was low. Surveyed maybe 30 or so of the ~80 sellers across all major geographies and product lines. 

A couple of observations:
- Generally, people don't want to learn new tools — even if it makes their life better or easier. 
- Lots of companies suffer from "if we buy it, they will come" syndrome; a lot of thought needs to be put into building the desire—in both individuals and the collective—to use the tool. 
- Having "power users" is key — power users find joy in learning about a product and often have a desire to share that with others. No enablement strategy is better than a really hyped employee recording a loom explaining why tool XYZ will change their lives (coming from the boss doesn't count for the same reason why listening to our parents' advice is harder than listening to a friend's)
voice_reason: Highly characteristic. Opens with specific, grounded detail (Gainsight, ~30 of ~80 sellers) that earns authority through evidence. The parenthetical humor is dry and observational ('coming from the boss doesn't count for the same reason why listening to our parents' advice is harder than listening to a friend's'). 'If we buy it, they will come' syndrome is a specific, vivid naming pattern. No hedging — assertions are direct ('No enablement strategy is better than...'). The casual specificity ('maybe 30 or so', 'recording a loom') feels human and anti-corporate. Strong training signal across multiple voice dimensions.

text:
We use conversational coaching to simultaneously build the self-advocacy muscle and excavate their professional history. 

We turn that data into a "3D talent graph" that captures both attributes + context. 

Best part is we capture qualitative insights (values, mindset, soft skills) that previously couldn't be collected or leveraged at scale.

text:
I think there are times when I annoy my coworkers where they would rather be dealing with an AI version of me!

On a more serious note, I've set up something similar for myself - not for off-loading decision-making but rather as a way to ensure consistency in my operations and access novel capabilities that would be too time intensive or onerous to otherwise exercise myself e.g vetting key decisions to ensure consistency in strategy, leveraging a custom "socratic tutor" that helps me flesh out my thinking, generating first-pass content for dissemination, etc.

That said, I could never hand-off authority to an AI-Vrijen -- for me, the chokepoint is my own ability to comprehensively share all the necessary context for making major decisions not to mention the effort associated with keeping that information up-to-date with day-to-day changes, both of which are already difficult without putting my company's future in the hands of a machine. 

I also think it's a little ethically sketchy -- I'm in this role because there is the expectation that I bring my best capabilities to the table. To the extent that the buck ultimately stops with me, I couldn't justify abdicating responsibility to the AI entirely.

text:
Guess who's got two thumbs and is already building that with you? 

Ultimately, I couldn't agree with you more. It's a stupid rat race and I also hate that we have to package Sisyphean advice into LinkedIn posts and tell people to do it all with a smile.

Self-deprecating humor lands naturally ('two thumbs'), the prison/blueprint metaphor is specific and vivid, 'Sisyphean advice' shows intellectual vocabulary deployed casually. The shift from philosophical frustration to playful ribbing ('roasting me about the posts Logan forces me to do') is authentic and human. No hedging — 'I couldn't agree with you more' is direct, 'That Growth Mindset bit was terrible' owns the L without softening. The winky face and casual run-on at the end feel like real conversational rhythm, not performed informality. Strong training signal for warmth-through-substance and dry observational humor.

text:
This jobs market is an arms race.

Candidates who rarely get past the ATS resort to a "numbers game" approach where Easy Apply/auto-apply tools are attractive. Employers are flooded by applicants and turn to more technology to help them.

It remains to be seen who will actually come out on top, but I suspect what we need is a third way that re-aligns the shitty incentives currently at play.

Good training signal for the direct-assertive-substance pattern.

text:
Brian Bies I appreciate your support. For me, the following elements tend to be key:
1) Prioritized search - start with existing connections that you can easily re-initiate contact with, move on to 2nd degree connections for whom you can receive a warm introduction, and  then turn to cold outreach if necessary. Following this pattern tends to be useful even if you ultimate need cold outreach because it gives you a lot of great practice before the real challenge
2) Personalize outreach - this boils down to finding points of commonality with someone. The more recent the better. Use this as a wedge to grab their attention. More importantly, no one likes a generic message that they think you've sent to a dozen people before
3) Reference relevant parts of your background - Briefly mention the best, most relevant parts of your profile in the email. 1-2 lines should be sufficient. We're more likely to help people we're impressed by.
voice_reason: Clear personal voice throughout. Direct and specific with practical frameworks (prioritized search hierarchy). Earns authority through evidence ('following this pattern tends to be useful because...'). Human warmth without corporate speak ('no one likes a generic message'). The closing line 'We're more likely to help people we're impressed by' is a great voice marker — assertive, honest, slightly dry. Minor ding: the numbered list format and a few phrases like 'boils down to' are slightly generic, but overall strong training signal with distinctive rhythm and substance-first communication style.

But the sample is very short and 'wonderful to see you succeed' and 'Congratulations!' are generic. There's a flicker of voice in the framing but not enough texture or distinctive language to be a strong training signal.
